description: This image is an electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD) grain boundary map of Fe-8 wt% Al low-density ferritic steel after hot deformation, with a 500 μm scale bar and black arrows indicating the applied shear deformation direction. Black lines represent high-angle grain boundaries (misorientation ≥15°), while red lines represent low-angle subgrain boundaries (misorientation 2° ≤ θ <15°). The microstructure exhibits original grains elongated along the shear direction, with extensive subgrain formation near grain boundaries, corresponding to dynamic recrystallization evolution under simulated hot rolling conditions (900-1100℃, strain rate 0.1-10 s⁻¹, immediately quenched post-deformation to preserve high-temperature microstructure). This visualization supports differentiation of continuous and discontinuous dynamic recrystallization mechanisms, showing grain boundary misorientation evolution correlated to recrystallization texture development, which identifies process windows for reducing deep drawing roping defects in hot rolled Fe-Al steel sheets.

This image displays a grain boundary characterization map of Fe-8wt%Al low-density ferritic steel after hot deformation, with a 500 μm scale bar provided. Black and red lines are assigned to high-angle grain boundaries (θ≥15°) and low-angle subgrain boundaries (2°≤θ<15°) respectively, while the two opposite arrows mark the shear deformation direction during hot torsion tests that simulate hot rolling conditions. The observed microstructure features initial ferrite grains elongated along the shear direction, with subgrains and newly nucleated recrystallized grains formed near original grain boundaries, reflecting dynamic recrystallization evolution. This microstructure evidence supports the core conclusion that dynamic recrystallization mechanisms (continuous or discontinuous) can be regulated by adjusting hot deformation parameters, and discontinuous dynamic recrystallization that yields weak random texture is beneficial to reduce roping defects in subsequent deep drawing of the steel sheets.

This figure displays an electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD) grain boundary map of Fe-8wt%Al low-density ferritic steel after hot deformation, with a provided scale bar of 500 μm. Black lines in the map correspond to high-angle grain boundaries (misorientation ≥15°), while red lines represent low-angle subgrain boundaries (misorientation between 2° and 15°). The characterized samples are processed by hot torsion simulation at 900-1100 °C under strain rates ranging from 0.1 s⁻¹ to 10 s⁻¹, followed by immediate quenching to preserve the high-temperature deformed microstructure. The microstructure shows a combination of elongated deformed grains, subgrains formed by dislocation rearrangement, and fine equiaxed recrystallized grains, which is applied to identify the operating dynamic recrystallization mechanisms (continuous DRX and discontinuous DRX) under different thermomechanical conditions. This figure supports the core conclusion that discontinuous dynamic recrystallization generates randomly oriented recrystallized grains with weak texture, which is beneficial to suppress roping defects during subsequent deep drawing of the hot-rolled steel sheets.

This micrograph presents the initial fully recrystallized equiaxed ferritic microstructure of Fe-8wt%Al low-density ferritic steel, with a 500 μm scale bar for dimensional reference. The sample is fabricated via three-pass hot rolling with a cumulative true strain of 1, yielding 100% recrystallized, isotropic single-phase A2-structured ferrite grains with relatively uniform size distribution. This microstructure serves as the uniform baseline for subsequent hot torsion tests performed under 900-1100℃ and strain rates of 0.1-10 s⁻¹, which simulate hot rolling conditions to explore continuous and discontinuous dynamic recrystallization mechanisms. The figure supports that the consistent initial recrystallized microstructure eliminates pre-existing texture interference, ensuring reliable identification of dynamic recrystallization process windows to optimize rolling processes for reducing deep-drawing roping defects of the alloy.

This figure displays the grain boundary misorientation distribution of Fe-8%Al low-density ferritic steel after hot deformation, characterized via electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD). The horizontal axis represents misorientation angle (°), while the vertical axis shows the frequency of each misorientation value. A red vertical line at 15° distinguishes low angle boundaries (LAB, <15°) and high angle boundaries (HAB, ≥15°). Quantified results indicate LAB accounts for 85% of total grain boundaries, HAB accounts for 15%, and the average misorientation angle is 9.4°. The frequency declines sharply with increasing misorientation angle, with most values concentrated below 10°, which demonstrates the dominance of low-angle subgrain boundaries generated during dynamic recovery and continuous dynamic recrystallization (CDRX). This result validates the mechanism that subgrain boundaries gradually transform into high-angle boundaries during CDRX under appropriate hot deformation conditions.

This plot presents the grain boundary misorientation angle frequency distribution of Fe-8 wt% Al low-density ferritic steel after hot deformation, characterized by electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD). A red vertical line at 15° divides low-angle boundaries (LAB, misorientation <15°) and high-angle boundaries (HAB, misorientation ≥15°): LAB accounts for 47% of total grain boundaries, HAB accounts for 53%, and the average misorientation angle is 23.7°. The curve shows that misorientation angles below 5° have the highest frequency, which decreases rapidly as the angle increases, and maintains a low stable level when the angle is above 15°. This distribution reflects the transformation from low-angle subgrain boundaries to high-angle grain boundaries during dynamic recrystallization, supporting the dynamic recrystallization mechanism analysis of the tested steel under hot rolling conditions.

This figure consists of one reference {110} pole figure (subplot a) and four experimental {110} pole figures (subplots b-e) characterizing texture evolution of Fe-8%Al bcc ferritic low-density steel after hot torsion deformation. Subplot a displays standard ideal orientations commonly observed in bcc alloy torsion tests, with labeled orientation components: D1 {112̅}<111>, D2 {1̅1̅2}<111>, J1 {01̅1}<2̅11>, F {110}<001>, E1 {011̅}<111>, E2 {01̅1}<111>, J2 {11̅0}<11̅2>. Subplots b-e correspond to deformed samples at true strain ε=2 (max texture intensity 2.50), ε=5 (max 2.46), ε=15 (max 3.43), ε=25 (max 3.17), respectively. The texture intensity first increases with increasing strain to a peak at ε=15, then slightly decreases at ε=25, with dominant orientations consistent with standard bcc torsion texture components. This data supports analysis of dynamic recrystallization texture evolution under hot deformation conditions for the studied alloy.

This group of electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD) maps presents the microstructures of Fe-8wt%Al low-density ferritic steel samples after torsion deformation with true strain ε=2 at 1100℃. Subplot a corresponds to the sample deformed at a strain rate of 5 s⁻¹, while subplot b corresponds to the sample deformed at 10 s⁻¹, both with a 500 μm scale bar. Black lines represent grain boundaries, and red lines mark low-angle grain boundaries, deformation bands or deformation-induced defects. Comparison shows that the sample under higher strain rate (10 s⁻¹) has much denser red deformation features and more prominent localized deformation zones than the 5 s⁻¹ sample. These results demonstrate the influence of strain rate on hot deformation defect formation and dynamic recrystallization behavior of the experimental steel, supporting that dynamic recrystallization mechanisms can be regulated via strain rate adjustment to optimize hot-rolled microstructure and reduce subsequent deep drawing defects.

This figure presents grain boundary misorientation distribution functions of Fe-8%Al low-density ferritic steel deformed at 1100 °C under different strain rates, obtained via electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD) characterization. Subplot a corresponds to a strain rate of 5 s⁻¹, while subplot b corresponds to 10 s⁻¹. In both plots, the black line shows experimentally measured misorientation frequency, the blue line is the theoretical MacKenzie distribution for randomly oriented cubic crystals, and the vertical red line demarcates low-angle boundaries (LAB, <15°, left) and high-angle boundaries (HAB, ≥15°, right). For 5 s⁻¹, LAB accounts for 20%, HAB for 80%, with an average misorientation angle of 33.1°. For 10 s⁻¹, LAB accounts for 22%, HAB for 78%, with an average misorientation angle of 32.9°. Both experimental distributions are close to the MacKenzie random distribution with a peak near 45°, indicating discontinuous dynamic recrystallization (DDRX) dominates under these high strain rate conditions, producing nearly randomly oriented recrystallized grains with low LAB fraction, confirming dynamic recovery is suppressed at high strain rates.

This figure presents paired EBSD microstructure maps and associated {110} pole figures for Fe-8wt%Al low-density ferritic steel deformed at high strain rates, with recrystallized grains (internal misorientation <2°) highlighted in blue in each microstructure map. Subfigure a corresponds to a deformation strain rate of 5 s⁻¹, its {110} pole figure has a maximum texture intensity of 1.77. Subfigure b corresponds to a higher strain rate of 10 s⁻¹, its {110} pole figure has a lower maximum texture intensity of 1.66. The decreasing maximum texture intensity with increasing strain rate indicates that recrystallized grains have more random orientations at higher strain rates, which is consistent with the activation of discontinuous dynamic recrystallization (DDRX) at higher strain rates, a mechanism that produces weak random texture beneficial for reducing roping defects during subsequent deep drawing of the hot-rolled steel sheets.

This is a strain rate-temperature map for Fe–8 wt% Al ferritic steel, plotting the activation domain of discontinuous dynamic recrystallization (DDRX) during hot deformation. The x-axis is temperature ranging from 850 °C to 1150 °C, the y-axis is strain rate in logarithmic scale from 0.01 s⁻¹ to 100 s⁻¹. Green dots represent conditions where DDRX is not activated (continuous dynamic recrystallization, CDRX, operates instead), red dots represent DDRX activated conditions, and the dashed black line marks the transition boundary between the two mechanisms. Tested deformation conditions cover temperatures of 900 °C, 1000 °C, 1100 °C, and strain rates from 0.1 s⁻¹ to 10 s⁻¹. The map shows that higher temperature lowers the critical strain rate required for DDRX activation. This figure confirms the competition between CDRX and DDRX as a function of hot deformation parameters, defines the process window for DDRX, which supports the strategy of adjusting hot rolling parameters to obtain random texture and reduce deep drawing roping defects.
